title: CPR and AED readiness checklist
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for preparing the group to call help, start CPR training steps, and find an AED. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
content: Use this entry to organize preparing the group to call help, start CPR training steps, and find an AED. Start with scene safety, hand hygiene, calm communication, and written timing. Keep the person comfortable, protect privacy, avoid risky experiments, and prepare a clear handover for a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, or trained first-aider. Watch especially for: unresponsive and not breathing normally, no AED access, unsafe floor, or exhausted rescuer. In a long emergency, review the person regularly instead of assuming they are stable.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “CPR and AED readiness checklist”.
soap or sanitizer if available — for «CPR and AED readiness checklist»
gloves or clean barrier if available — for «CPR and AED readiness checklist»
paper and pen. Context: “CPR and AED readiness checklist”.
phone or radio if available — for «CPR and AED readiness checklist»
labels for times and medicines — for «CPR and AED readiness checklist»
steps: Check that the place is safe for the helper and the injured or sick person. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Call emergency help or send a reliable person for help when any red flag is present. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Write the time the problem started, what changed, medicines taken, allergies, and known conditions. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Use clean hands and clean barriers before touching wounds, body fluids, food, medicines, infants, or medical devices. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Keep the person warm or cool as appropriate, still if trauma is possible, and protected from crowds. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Recheck and record changes regularly so deterioration is noticed early. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
Prepare a short handover note with name, age, symptoms, times, medicines, allergies, and actions already taken. Apply it specifically within the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” procedure.
warnings: Do not use this as a replacement for training, emergency services, a clinician, or local medical guidance. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “CPR and AED readiness checklist”.

Do not guess medicine doses, mix medicines without label checking, share prescriptions, or perform invasive procedures. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “CPR and AED readiness checklist”.
Escalate urgently if you see: unresponsive and not breathing normally, no AED access, unsafe floor, or exhausted rescuer.
Keep children, elders, pregnancy, disability, immune weakness, and chronic illness in the high-risk group. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “CPR and AED readiness checklist”.
common_mistakes: Waiting too long after red flags appear while carrying out “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Not writing times and medicines while carrying out “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Touching wounds or fluids without hand hygiene while carrying out “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Letting crowds block air, privacy, or transport while carrying out “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Assuming a quiet person is stable without rechecking while carrying out “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
alternatives: Use official first-aid training when available as a fallback for “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Use local emergency numbers and pharmacists for medicine questions as a fallback for “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Use paper handover notes when phones are dead as a fallback for “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Use a trusted translator if language blocks care as a fallback for “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
failure_signs: Red flags appear: unresponsive and not breathing normally, no AED access, unsafe floor, or exhausted rescuer
The person becomes harder to wake, breathe, drink, speak, walk, or recognize during “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
Pain, bleeding, swelling, fever, confusion, or weakness gets worse during “CPR and AED readiness checklist”
when_not_to_use: Do not delay emergency care to complete the checklist when using the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” record
Do not move a trauma patient unless staying is more dangerous when using the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” record
Do not force care on a conscious person except immediate life safety situations under local rules when using the “CPR and AED readiness checklist” record
short_term: Stabilize the scene, reduce immediate harm, record key facts, and connect the person to trained help. This note applies specifically to CPR and AED readiness checklist.
long_term: Keep medical records updated, train the group in certified first aid, maintain supplies, and practice respectful handovers. This note applies specifically to CPR and AED readiness checklist.
if_method_fails: Escalate to emergency services, nearest clinic, pharmacist, radio medical consult, or evacuation route; document the failed step and time. This note applies specifically to CPR and AED readiness checklist.
environment_notes: In apartments, heat, smoke, poor ventilation, crowds, no elevator, and water outages can make medical problems worse. Plan quiet space, clean water, light, and transport. This note applies specifically to CPR and AED readiness checklist.
